summary: Martin Fowler describes the 'snowflake server' anti-pattern where production servers become unique, manually-configured systems that are difficult to reproduce, understand, or modify. These servers accumulate cruft and undocumented changes over time, making them fragile and resistant to upgrades. The solution is to define server configurations as automated recipes using tools like Puppet or Chef, stored in version control. This approach enables reproducibility, auditability, easier testing environments, and supports continuous delivery practices.
key_points: Snowflake servers are unique configurations created through manual, ad-hoc changes that become impossible to reliably reproduce
The fragility of snowflakes manifests most severely when changes are needed - upgrades cause unpredictable knock-on effects and debugging becomes stressful
Automated configuration recipes (Puppet, Chef) provide both reproducibility AND understandability, which disk images alone cannot offer
Forcing all configuration changes through version-controlled recipes creates an excellent audit mechanism for regulated environments
Avoiding snowflakes enables test environments to be true clones of production, reducing configuration-related bugs
money_quotes: The result is a unique snowflake - good for a ski resort, bad for a data center.
Another metaphor I've heard for this is that you should treat your servers like cattle and not like pets.
Snowflakes soon become hard to understand and modify. Upgrades of one bit software cause unpredictable knock-on effects.
You're not sure what parts of the configuration are important, or just the way it came out of the box many years ago.

The point of using a recipe is not just that you can easily rebuild the server (which you could also do with imaging) but you can also easily understand its configuration and thus modify it more easily.
This fragility leads to long, stressful bouts of debugging.
This is one reason why you often see important software running on ancient operating systems.
key_insight: Server configurations should be defined as version-controlled, automated recipes rather than accumulated manual changes, because reproducibility without understandability still leaves you with a fragile system.
